coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots based magnetic nanoparticles as a promising delivery system for controlled doxorubicin release
coredata.dc:creator: Javadian, Soheila
Najafi, Khadijeh
Sadrpoor, S. Morteza
Ektefa, Fatemeh
Dalir, Nima
Nikkhah, Maryam
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a nanocarrier was prepared for targeted delivery of doxorubicin (DOX), as a drug model, to cancer cells. To this end, nontoxic Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) were first synthesized by the co-precipitation method. The NPs then underwent surface functionalization by hydrophilic and biocompatible polyethylene glycol (PEG) to improve their stability. The Fe3O4@PEG was eventually decorated by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for imparting specific optical properties and increase its drug loading capacity. The obtained nanocarriers (Fe3O4@PEG@GQD) exhibited low toxicity, hydrodynamic diameter of 129 nm, and a drug loading content of 27%, along with superior superparamagnetic properties. Also, the morphology of nanocarrier was surveyed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The drug release studies indicated a pH-dependent profile where higher release rates were observed at acidic pH (5.0) as compared with the physiological pH (7.4). The anticancer activity of Fe3O4@PEG@GQD-DOX was comparable to free drugs in the case of human breast cancer cells. The introduced nano-drug delivery system can be considered as a promising candidate for the controlled release of DOX. Finally, quantum mechanical computations were employed in a better understanding of drug release mechanism. The results showed a decline in the adsorption energy of the drug on GQDs from −107.1 kJ mol−1 at pH 7 to −66.2 kJ mol−1 at pH 5. Moreover, the HOMO-LUMO calculation and the electrostatic surface potential method were applied to further explore the drug-GQDs interactions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Controlled release
Drug delivery
Graphene quantum dots
Fickian transport
Quantum computation
And adsorption energy

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was made possible through financial support from the Tarbiat Modares University

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FTIR spectra of the Fe3O4 NPs, Fe3O4@PEG and Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs; (b) TEM image of Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs; (c) UV–Vis spectra of the Fe3O4 NPs, Fe3O4@PEG and Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs; (d) room temperature magnetization curve as a function of applied magnetic field of the Fe3O4 NPs and Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs and (e) TGA curves of the Fe3O4 NPs and Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs.
(a) DLC and (b) DLE of Fe3O4@PEG and Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs as a function of DOX/ nanocarriers ratio.
Drug release profile of Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs and the values are mean ± SD (n = 3).
Cytotoxicity of (a) Fe3O4@PEG@GQD and (b) Free DOX and Fe3O4@PEG@GQD-DOX at different concentrations of DOX the values are mean ± SD (n = 3).
DOX adsorption onto GQD at (a) pH 5, (b) pH 7.
ESP figures for (a) DOX, (b) GQD at pH 7, (c) complex at pH 7, (d) GQD at pH 5, and (e) complex at pH 5.
Magnetic based drug delivery systems (DDSs) for DOX delivery.
Kinetic model parameters for test samples; n: kinetic exponent, R2: regression coefficient.

The values of the HOMO, LUMO, energy gap, global hardness, electronic chemical potential, and overall stabilization energy for different compounds (all upon eV), and fractional number of electrons transfer(dimensionless).

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots based magnetic nanoparticles as a promising delivery system for controlled doxorubicin release

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O. Box 14115-175, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physical Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O. Box 14115-175, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanobiotechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, P. O. box 14115-175, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanobiotechnology, Faculty of Biological Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, P. O. box 14115-175, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a nanocarrier was prepared for targeted delivery of doxorubicin (DOX), as a drug model, to cancer cells. To this end, nontoxic Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) were first synthesized by the co-precipitation method. The NPs then underwent surface functionalization by hydrophilic and biocompatible polyethylene glycol (PEG) to improve their stability. The Fe3O4@PEG was eventually decorated by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for imparting specific optical properties and increase its drug loading capacity. The obtained nanocarriers (Fe3O4@PEG@GQD) exhibited low toxicity, hydrodynamic diameter of 129 nm, and a drug loading content of 27%, along with superior superparamagnetic properties. Also, the morphology of nanocarrier was surveyed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The drug release studies indicated a pH-dependent profile where higher release rates were observed at acidic pH (5.0) as compared with the physiological pH (7.4). The anticancer activity of Fe3O4@PEG@GQD-DOX was comparable to free drugs in the case of human breast cancer cells. The introduced nano-drug delivery system can be considered as a promising candidate for the controlled release of DOX. Finally, quantum mechanical computations were employed in a better understanding of drug release mechanism. The results showed a decline in the adsorption energy of the drug on GQDs from −107.1 kJ mol−1 at pH 7 to −66.2 kJ mol−1 at pH 5. Moreover, the HOMO-LUMO calculation and the electrostatic surface potential method were applied to further explore the drug-GQDs interactions.

ce:section.ce:para: As the second cause of death in the world, cancer accounted for an estimated 9.6 million deaths in 2018 [1]. Among cancer treatment methods, chemotherapy is often used in 50% of cases [2]. The main defect of this method is relatively low drug delivery efficacy accompanied by unintended penetration of the drugs into non-target cells and tissues [2]. In recent decades, like other scientific fields, smart and multi-functional nano-systems have been considered in the targeted treatment of cancer [3]. In the development of these nano-systems, a combination of polymers, metal or metal oxides nanoparticles (NPs), quantum dots, liposomes and etc. with different therapeutic approaches, based on optical light or magnetic fields, are utilized.

Recently, iron oxide NPs, particularly magnetite (Fe3O4), have been widely used in a variety of biomedical usages such as magnetic resonance imaging contrast agents [4], magnetic drug delivery [5], hyperthermia [6], biosensors [7] and bio-labeling [8]. For the mentioned applications, Fe3O4 NPs should possess a narrow size distribution and good dispersibility in aqueous media [9]. In the field of drug delivery, the magnetic properties can greatly contribute to increasing the anticancer efficacy and reducing the side effects. Using an external local magnetic field, drug-loaded magnetic NPs could be easily gathered in the tumor and release the drug [10,11]. Generally, the surface charge of NPs plays an influential role in its suspension stability. The isoelectric point of Fe3O4 NPs lies on pH~7. This means that particles will aggregate and precipitate at physiological conditions [12]. On the other hand, the ferrofluid introduced into the blood circulation must be protected from the immune system and phagocytic cells [13]. Numerous studies have shown that to prolong the circulation time, the colloidal particles should be as small as possible (below 200 nm), made from natural materials, and possess a neutral or hydrophilic surface. These requirements can be met by coating the NPs surfaces with hydrophilic polymers, such as chitosan, dextran and polyethylene glycol (PEG) or lipids, proteins, and dendrimers [14,15]. Also, the long chain of polymers can prevent the aggregation of NPs through steric effect. Moreover, surface modifications can endow with considerable properties such as drug loading and fluorescent tags [14]. PEG seems to be one of the most appropriate candidates for this purpose due to its hydrophilicity, high water-solubility, biocompatibility, nonantigenic, and protein-resistant properties, which can prolong its blood circulation duration [12,16]. Furthermore, the approval of PEG by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) led to its application as an excipient in numerous pharmaceutical formulation [16]. Recent studies have reported the surface modification of the magnetic NPs with hydrophilic substances such as glutamic acid [17], carboxyl functionalized reduced graphene oxide [18], carboxymethyl chitosan [19], and lipid [20] for doxorubicin (DOX) loading. Achieving high drug-loading capacity (DLC) combined with multifunctional properties such as optical or magnetic properties is a challenging issue in preparing hybrid structures for diagnosis and treatment of cancer. Graphene-based materials are a popular group of multifunctional substances for biomedical applications due to their unrivaled properties, such as great thermal and optical adsorption capabilities, in-vivo low toxicity and single-atom layered structure facilitating its modification. In specific, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as zero-dimensional nanomaterial, exhibit high photoluminescent quantum yields, excellent photostability, and biocompatibility [21,22]. Regarding the aromatic structure of most anticancer drugs, GQDs can increment the DLC efficiency through π-π interaction and promote the traceability of the nanocarriers.

In the present work, a nontoxic and pH-sensitive multifunctional hybrid structure, comprising DOX-encapsulated Fe3O4@PEG@GQD, was synthesized and characterized to be used as a theranostic agent for cancer diagnosis and therapy. The in vitro drug release profile, cellular uptake, cytotoxicity toward cancer cells, and blood compatibility of the Fe3O4@PEG@GQD were also explored. Moreover, the density functional theory (DFT) framework was utilized to further elucidate the drug release mechanism and the involved parameters. For this purpose, several factors such as adsorption energy, hydrogen bonding, electrostatic surface potential (ESP), the energy of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and the fractional number of electrons transfer were investigated.

FeSO4.7H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, NH4OH, (25%), polyethylene glycol 4000, citric acid, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), sodium acetate buffer and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich Co and were used as-received. The human breast adenocarcinoma cell line (MCF-7) was supplied from the Iranian Biological Resource Center. DOX-hydrochloride was purchased from EBEWE Pharma.

Magnetite Fe3O4 NPs were fabricated by a co-precipitation reaction. For this purpose, a mixture of Fe2+/Fe3+ at a molar ratio of 1:2 in deionized water was poured into a Twin-neck flask under argon atmosphere. The mixture was stirred at 40 °C for a certain period of time to dissolve the salts, and then the ammonia solution was added dropwise to the solution until pH reached 11. The solution was then stirred at 70 °C for 50 min. Black precipitate was collected by magnetic decantation and repeatedly washed with distilled water [23].

An aqueous solution of PEG (50% w/v) was added to the Fe3O4 NPs and then was placed in an ultrasonic probe device at room temperature for 3 min - 3 one-minute pulses operating at 3–5 W. After magnetic separation and washing the solid product for several times, it was dried at room temperature.

The PEG-coated Fe3O4 NPs were added to a quantum dot solution synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid followed by 24 h of stirring [24]. The product was separated with a super-magnet and vacuum-dried at 70 °C.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (Nicolet IR 100 FT-IR Spectrometer) was used for the functional groups evaluation of NPs. The crystalline phases were recorded using X-ray diffraction (XRD, X'Pert MPD, Philips Co. Ltd., Holland) with Co Kα radiation. The shape and morphology of the synthesized nanocarriers were checked with a PHILIPS CM300 transmission electron microscope (TEM). The Magnetic properties of the particles were explored using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM, Meghnatis Daghigh Kavir Co. Ltd., Iran). The hydrodynamic sizes of the samples were detected by dynamic light scattering (DLS, Zetasizer, Malvern Instruments Co. Ltd., UK). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, BAHR STA 503, Germany) was used to assess the thermal degradation of the hybrid system. The surface area was estimated with Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis (High precision Surface area and pure size analyzer BELSORP mini 2). The DOX content was determined by measuring UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy (Rayleigh - Model UV2601 UV/VIS - Double Beam Spectrophotometer).

The doxorubicin hydrochloride solution was added to Fe3O4@PEG@GQD dispersed in PBS (pH 7.4) with 1:1 M ratio. The mixture was stirred in the dark (37 °C - 24 h). To estimate the DOX content of the nanocarriers, according to the (1) and (2) equations, Fe3O4@PEG@GQD-DOX was first separated using a magnet, and the optical absorption of its supernatant was measured at 485 nm.
(1)
drug loading capacity
DLC
% =
a
a + b
∗ 100
(2)
drug loading efficiency
DLE
% =
a c
∗ 100
In which,a = weight of the loaded drug.b = weight of carrier.c = weight of the added drug.

The in vitro release of DOX was investigated in PBS (pH 7.4, Similar to the physiological environment) and acetate buffer (pH 5.0, Similar to inside the tumor). For this purpose, 1 mg Fe3O4@PEG@GQD-DOX was suspended in 2.5 mL of PBS and transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO 12000 Da). The end-sealed dialysis bag was placed into 25 mL of PBS at 37 °C under continuous shaking at 100 rpm. Every two hours, 0.8 mL of external buffer was sampled, and replaced with an equal volume of fresh buffer. The absorbance of the samples was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 485 nm. The cumulative release percentage (%) was determined by dividing the concentrations of DOX released by initial loaded DOX concentrations. The same process was repeated for acetate buffer. The release experiments were conducted in triplicate.

To further survey the drug release at both pH values, different kinetic models were selected to explain the kinetic release (eq. 3–6) [25].
(3)
zero − order model
f t
=
k 0
t
(4)
First − order model
ln
1 −
f t
=
k 1
t
(5)
Higuchi model
f t
=
k H
t 0.5
(6)
Korsmeyer – Peppas model
f t
=
k p
t n

In all the above equations, ft is the fraction of the released drug (DOX) (at the time of t), the rate constants were denoted by k0 and k1for the zero- and first-order models, respectively. kH and kp show the rate constants for the Higuchi and Korsmeyer-Peppas models, respectively. The exponent release is denoted by n whose value indicates the release mechanism. For the spherical particles, n ≤ 0.43 represents diffusion-controlled release, 0.43 < n < 0.85 denotes to both swelling- and diffusion-controlled release, and n ≥ 0.85 signifies relaxation-controlled release.

In vitro cytocompatibility of the Fe3O4@PEG@GQD was assessed using MTT assay. For this purpose, breast cancer MCF7 cells were maintained in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) under standard mammalian cell culture conditions. The cell cultures were kept under 5.0% CO2 humidified atmosphere at 37 °C and grown to 80% of confluency. Then, the cells were transferred to sterile 96-well growth plates at 10,000 cells/well and 3 wells per treatment. The next day, the medium was exchanged, and the cells were treated with various concentrations (0, 10, 20, 30 μg L−1) of Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs. After the incubation period, 20.0 μL MTT was added to each well followed by incubation for another 4 h at 37 °C. The formazan crystals were dissolved in 100 μl/well of DMSO. The plate was shaken gently for 15 min and then the absorbance was measured using an ELISA microplate reader (μQuant and power wave Sx2, BioTek) at 570 nm.

Geometric optimization was performed for different pH values through DFT using M06-2× functionals and 6-31 g basis set in the water Solvent as implemented in Gaussian 09. The adsorption energy was calculated by:
(7)
E adsorption
=
E
GQD − DOX
−
E GQD
+
E DOX

The electrostatic surface potential was plotted by GaussView 6. The hydrogen bonding was applied to the atoms in the molecule (AIM) by AIM 2000 package, and its corresponding energy was calculated by EHB = 0.5 V(r) [26], which V(r) denotes the local potential energy. The energy gap (Eg), chemical hardness (η), electronic chemical potential (μ), overall stabilization energy (∆ESE(AB)), and fractional number of electrons transfer (∆N) were calculated according to the calculation methods mentioned in Hesabi and Ali reports [27,28] (as listed in Table S1 in supporting information).

The crystalline structure of the synthesized iron oxide NPs was confirmed by XRD analysis (supporting information Fig. S1). A comparison of the XRD pattern with the JCPDS data (card no. 19–0629) showed that the as-synthesized NPs were magnetite (Fe3O4) with an inverse spinel structure [29].

Fig. 1a demonstrates the FT-IR spectra of the synthesized nanocarrier at different stages. The pristine Fe3O4 NPs showed characteristic bands related to the Fe − O vibrations of spinel structure tetrahedral sites at about 620 cm−1 (shoulder) and 572 cm−1 [30]. The similar peaks have been observed in the cases of Fe3O4@PEG, Fe3O4@PEG@GQD, and Fe3O4@PEG@GQD-DOX. A differentiable C − H stretching at around 2885 cm−1 and a sharp C − O stretching band at 1105 cm−1 in Fe3O4@PEG spectrum can be assigned to PEG molecules on the surface of Fe3O4 NPs [17]. The spectrum of GQDs exhibited a wide peak related to OH stretching vibration (2800–3500 cm−1), while those emerged at 1700 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 corresponded to CO and C–OH stretching vibration of the carboxyl group. Other peaks at 1080 cm−1 are the sign of C-OH stretching vibrations [18]. Adsorption of DOX on Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs can be confirmed by the appearance of the peaks around 1278, 1416, 1627, and 1718 cm−1, corresponding to C − O − C, aromatic CC, bending N − H, and CO bonds in DOX, respectively. A sharp peak at 3440 cm−1, in addition to the O − H bond, could be attributed to the stretching vibration of first-type DOX amines [3]. TEM image of Fe3O4@PEG@GQD is depicted in Fig. 1b. As shown, the spherical magnetic NPs are surrounded by light background GQDs. The UV–Vis spectroscopy was used to further characterize the formation of nanocarrier and investigate the efficiency of drug loading. For Fe3O4 NPs, no obvious absorption peaks were observed in (Fig. 1c). GQDs show a typical absorption peak at 340 nm [31], which is somehow visible in the Fe3O4@PEG@GQD spectrum. The characteristic absorption peak of free DOX at 485 nm was also detected in Fe3O4@PEG@GQD-DOX, which further confirmed the successful adsorption of DOX.

The zeta potential of the Fe3O4)synthesized by the coprecipitation method in alkaline medium(was −20 mV, which showed an increment to −30 mV upon the addition of polymer coating (Fe3O4@PEG). This parameter exhibited a slight increase to −33 mV (supporting information Fig. S3) due to interaction with GQDs. The average hydrodynamic size of the designed nanocarriers (Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs) was evaluated as 129 ± 10 nm.

The magnetization curves of Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs and Fe3O4 are presented in Fig. 1d. The magnetic field-moment measurements of the NPs were carried out at 298 K (room temperature). The obtained saturation magnetic moments were 74 and 35 emu g−1, for bare Fe3O4 NPs and Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs, respectively. The decrease in saturation magnetic moments indicates the formation of a non-magnetic shell on the NPs. Moreover, the absence of a hysteresis loop supports the superparamagnetic behavior, zero magnetization upon the removal of the applied magnetic field, of the synthesized NPs which is of a crucial importance for biomedical applications [32].

To evaluate the thermal stability of the Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs, TGA analysis was performed under nitrogen atmosphere from 25 °C to 850 °C at the heating rate of 10 °C min−1. The TGA curves of the Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs are shown in Fig. 1e. The absolute weight loss of the bare Fe3O4 was 6.2% (Fig. 1e) for the whole temperature range, which can be attributed to the evaporation of the physically and chemically adsorbed water. The TGA curve of the Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs sample was very different from that of Fe3O4 as it exhibited the three steps of weight loss. In the first step, 7% weight loss was detected from room temperature to 120 °C, which can be due to the removal of adsorbed water. The other two steps of weight loss at 120–290 °C (15%) and 290–530 °C (25%) were related to the decomposition of PEG and GQDs in the Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs hybrid, respectively [33,34].

Drug-loading capacity and efficiency are two important parameters in the development of nanocarriers. DLC represents the mass ratio of drugs relative to the nanocarriers, while the drug loading efficiency reflects the percentage of the drug that is successfully entrapped into the nanocarrier. The higher DLE and DLC offer more drug availability around the target site, thereby reducing the required dose along with the declining systemic side effects. The DLE, DLC of Fe3O4@PEG, and Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs are depicted in Fig. 2 relative to DOX/nanocarrier ratio. The UV absorption calibration curve is shown in the supporting information Fig. S3.

The results showed a significant enhancement in DLE and DLC by the incorporation of GQDs into Fe3O4@PEG. This effect can be explained by the π-π stackings between the graphene rings and the aromatic structure of the drug. Moreover, BET analysis suggested a significant increase in surface area from 67.0 ± 0.5 m2 g−1 for Fe3O4@PEG to 125.0 ± 0.9 m2 g−1 for Fe3O4@PEG@GQD.

An increase in DOX/nanocarrier ratio enhanced the DLC while decrementing DLE. The physical properties and DLC of some of the previously-reported magnetic nanocarriers are summarized in Table 1 . In parallel with favorable size and magnetic properties, the fabricated hybrid nano-system exhibited superior DLC.

The high rate of glycolysis in cancer cells increases the production of lactic acid, which leads to an acidic environment. With this in mind, pH-responsive drug delivery systems have attracted tremendous interest as “smart” drug delivery systems to overcome the limitations of the conventional drug formulations. Herein, the drug release pattern of the fabricated hybrid nano-system was evaluated at pH values of 5.0 and 7.4, representing acidic and physiological conditions, respectively. The cumulative DOX release profiles of Fe3O4@PEG@GQD-DOX are presented in Fig. 3 . During the first 36 h, approximately 75% of DOX was released under acidic conditions while the release was less than 21% at pH 7.4. Afterward, the drug release was steadily increased to 85% at 96 h. Moreover, in physiological conditions (pH 7.4) even after four days, most of the DOX molecules did not leave the carriers, which, in turn, may largely reduce the side effects of DOX on the normal tissues. Such a pH-sensitive drug release profile could be attributed to the weakening the DOX-GQDs bonding at acidic pH due to rise of protonated functional groups on DOX and GQD [40]. The quantum mechanical calculations in section 3.5 clarifies the release mechanism.

The kinetics of DOX release was explored by evaluating various models in terms of their regression coefficients. Table 2 reveals that the Korsmeyer-Peppas is the best kinetic model describing DOX release in natural and acidic conditions. The values of n (n = 0.21 at pH 5 and n = 0.11 at pH 7.4) determined for both pH conditions indicated the DOX release from Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs was controlled by diffusion, i.e., Fickian transport as confirmed by some previous works [41,42].

The toxicity of the hybrid nano-system on breast cancer cells was also addressed. For this purpose, MCF7 cells were incubated with Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs (0–30 μg L−1) for 24 h. The cell viability, as determined by MTT assay, demonstrated the negligible toxicity of Fe3O4@PEG@GQDs at all the tested concentrations (Fig. 4a). The cytotoxic efficiency of Fe3O4@PEG@GQD-DOX was expectedly found to be less than free DOX at the same drug concentration, which could be in part due to the gradual release of the drug from the carrier (Fig. 4b).

To better understand the release mechanism of DOX and its involved factors, quantum mechanical calculations were applied. Firstly the adsorption energy of the drug on GQD was investigated at both pHs. The results showed that the absolute adsorption energy of the drug on GQD decreased from −107.1 kJ mol−1 at pH 7 to −66.2 kJ mol−1 at pH 5. The lower absolute value of the adsorption energy (closer to zero) in an acidic environment indicates a decline in the attraction force of the drug and GQD, which increases the drug release at acidic environments as compared with neutral media. The drug adsorption energy results are consistent with the experimental findings. Fig. 5 presents the drug adsorption on the GQD at both neutral and acidic environments.

Since some studies showed that electrostatic interactions directly influence the drug release rates [43,44], the electrostatic surface potential (ESP) was examined to determine potential and charge distribution in the different compounds. Fig. 6 demonstrates the ESP of DOX, GQD, and complex (adsorbed DOX on GQD) at both pH values. The blue and red colors indicate positive and negative potential, respectively. In the neutral environment, the DOX had positive potential resulting from its ammonium group [45,46]) while GQD exhibited several sites with the negative potential. An attraction force between DOX and GQD can contribute to the DOX adsorption on GQD (Fig. 6a,b). Fig. 6c shows ESP of complex at pH 7 where some blue regions of the DOX were converted to yellow; suggesting the impact of GQD on the DOX. The regions with the positive and negative potential in the complex can help them to join each other, especially, the attraction force between the protonated ammonium group of DOX and the carboxylic acid group of GQD was reported in some other papers [47,48]. At an acidic environment, not only did the negative sites of GQD decrease (Fig. 6d), thereby declining the attraction force between DOX and GQD, but a repulsion force was also formed between DOX and GQD in several areas (Fig. 6e). The less attraction force at pH 5 can explain the decrease of complex adsorption energy at pH 5.

HOMO-LUMO calculation of DOX, GQD, and complexes, as well as their derived parameters such as Eg, η, and ΔN, can offer useful information on the stability and reactivity of different compounds. Fig. S4 shows the contribution of both HOMO and LUMO of DOX that mostly comes from the three aromatic rings. Also, HOMO and LUMO of different systems are displayed in Fig. S5-S8. HOMO of the complexes mainly focused on GQDs but their LUMO was more distributed on the DOX in neutral environment as compared with acidic conditions.

Compounds with higher energy gaps are kinetically more stable [45]. Table 3 displays a higher energy gap at pH 7 compared to pH 5. The decrease in Eg of complexes (compared to isolated DOX and GQDs) can be assigned to their interaction [49]. The lower energy gap of complex than both adsorbent and adsorbate was also reported in other studies [45,50]. As compounds with higher hardness values were more stable [51], the greater value of η at pH 7 indicated higher stability at pH 7. Moreover, the calculation of ΔN for two complexes suggested that when the GQD serves as a donor and DOX acts as an acceptor, the values of ΔN are positive; implying electron transfer from GQD to DOX. This result is in line with ESP findings (Fig. 6c and d) and μ (Table 3), where electron transfer occurred from compound with higher value μ to the species with lower μ [27]. An absolute value of ΔN is an index for better interaction [27]. Therefore, the complex had stronger interaction at pH 7 as it exhibited the higher absolute value of ΔN. Note the stronger interaction between DOX and GQD at pH 7 is in line with the adsorption energy results. Finally, the overall stabilization energy for both complexes was computed, and as its consequence the lower value of ΔΕSE(AB), the higher stability of the complex is at pH 7, which is in agreement with adsorption energy and experimental results.

The abundance of hydrogen and oxygen (in different functional groups) in both structures led to hydrogen bonding (HB) between both structures. The theory of atoms in molecules (AIM) is a common method for the evaluation of interactions nature, especially for HB. The total electronic density (ρ) and its Laplacian (∇2ρ) were calculated, and the HBs were determined according the studies of Sánchez-Viesca and popelier [52,53]. AIM analysis revealed two HBs between DOX and GQD at both pH values, and the strongest HB (63.8 kJ mol−1) was observed at pH 7, in agreement with the release results. The magnitude of ρ is another parameter reported as an index of HB strength [26]. According to Table S2, O100---H135 (at pH 7) showed the highest ρ, thereby offering the strongest HB.

The drug delivery system comprising of magnetite NPs modified with PEG and GQD was successfully prepared in this study for the controlled release of DOX. The size of Fe3O4@PEG@GQD-DOX was about 129 nm with high drug loading content. Loading efficiency of 71% and controlled drug release within 48 h were observed at acidic pH. The in vitro drug release rate was studied by the Korsmeyer-Peppas kinetic model which suggested that the DOX release of Fe3O4@PEG@GQD was controlled by diffusion, i.e., Fickian transport. Fe3O4@PEG@GQD-DOX is a promising magnetic drug delivery system with high potentials in targeted cancer therapy and other biomedical applications. The quantum mechanical computation results showed a significant decrement in the adsorption energy of drugs on GQDs at acidic conditions, which is in line with the experimental release results. ESP studies also suggested the decisive role of non-covalent interactions (especially electrostatic interaction) between GQD and drug in the controlled release of DOX.

KH.N., S.M.S., N.D. and S.J. designed the experiments. KH.N. performed the experiments and measurements. M.N. provided constructive advice for experiments. KH.N. wrote the first draft. S.J., F.E. and N.D. revised the manuscript. S.J. and M.N. supervised the research. All authors discussed the results and contributed to preparing the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the authors.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was made possible through financial support from the Tarbiat Modares University

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2021.115746.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: World cancer report: cancer research for cancer prevention, Lyon Int
